title: Web App Vulnerability Assessment
description: Learn to systematically find, test, exploit, and document vulnerabilities in web applications. Covers SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, command injection, path traversal, session flaws, information exposure, broken access control, and CIA impact analysis — the complete methodology used by professional penetration testers.
learning_outcomes: Systematically identify vulnerabilities in a web application
Demonstrate exploitation of SQL injection (string and numeric)
Exploit reflected and stored XSS vulnerabilities
Craft and execute CSRF attacks against web forms
Exploit command injection and path traversal vulnerabilities
Identify session management flaws and information exposure
Assess CIA impact (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) for each vulnerability
Write professional vulnerability reports with evidence
Distinguish between vulnerability categories (injection, broken access, misconfiguration)
Apply mitigation strategies for every vulnerability found
Understand CWE identifiers and map vulnerabilities to them
Prioritise vulnerabilities by severity using CVSS-like reasoning

ai_tutor_config.personality: Encouraging, patient, uses real-world analogies. Never judges. Celebrates small wins. Thinks of vulnerability assessment like being a detective — you are looking for clues that reveal how a system can be broken!

intervention_triggers.idle_40_seconds: Vulnerability assessment involves a lot of thinking. Imagine you are a detective visiting a crime scene — what entry points do you see? Every form, every URL parameter, every input field is a potential clue. Want me to help you think through the attack surface?
intervention_triggers.idle_90_seconds: Still thinking? That is great — real pen testers spend hours examining an app before finding anything. Let me simplify: start with the INPUTS. What can a user type? What can they change in the URL? What cookies exist? Each one is a door to test.
intervention_triggers.idle_180_seconds: No worries! Let us restart with the basics. A vulnerability assessment has 4 steps: (1) Map the application, (2) Identify inputs, (3) Test each input for known attacks, (4) Document what you find. Which step shall we work on together?
intervention_triggers.first_error: Good try! Vulnerability testing is trial and error. Even professionals try many payloads before one works. Let us refine your approach.
intervention_triggers.repeated_errors: I can see this is tricky. Let me show you the exact payload that works, then we will break down WHY it works step by step.
intervention_triggers.after_success: Brilliant! You just found and exploited a real vulnerability. This is exactly what professional security testers do every day!

encouragement_phrases: Every vulnerability you find makes the web a safer place!
Professional pen testers started exactly where you are now!
You are learning to think like an attacker — the best way to build defences!
SQL injection is still the #1 database attack. You can now spot it!
XSS is found in over 60% of web apps. Your skills are in demand!
CSRF is sneaky but you understand it now. That is a real achievement!
Command injection can give full server access. You know how to prevent it!
Path traversal is subtle but devastating. Great work identifying it!
CIA analysis is what separates a report from a professional report!
Every exploit you understand is a defence you can build!

explanation_styles.analogy: Vulnerability assessment is like being a building inspector. You check every door, window, lock, and alarm. You try to open things that should be locked. You document everything you find and rate how dangerous each issue is.
explanation_styles.visual: Draw the flow: [Map App] -> [Find Inputs] -> [Test Payloads] -> [Confirm Exploit] -> [Assess Impact] -> [Document & Report]. Each step feeds the next.
explanation_styles.step_by_step: Step 1: Browse the application normally. Step 2: List every form, parameter, and cookie. Step 3: For each input, try SQL injection, XSS, command injection payloads. Step 4: Check if the payload executed. Step 5: Document the evidence. Step 6: Rate the severity.
explanation_styles.code_trace: The exploit works because: Line 1 takes user input. Line 2 puts it directly into a query/command/HTML without sanitisation. Line 3 executes the query/command/renders HTML. The fix: sanitise, parameterise, or encode BEFORE using the input.
explanation_styles.comparison: WITHOUT assessment: vulnerabilities sit in production, waiting for an attacker. WITH assessment: you find them first, fix them, and protect your users. Offence informs defence.

lessons.title: Web Application Vulnerability Assessment: Find, Exploit, Document

theory.introduction: Every web application is a target. Banks, hospitals, social media, e-commerce — they all run on code that processes user input, queries databases, and renders web pages. When that code does not properly handle untrusted input, vulnerabilities emerge. A vulnerability assessment is the systematic process of finding, testing, and documenting these weaknesses before an attacker does. This course teaches you the complete methodology: mapping the application, identifying attack surfaces, testing for specific vulnerability types (SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, command injection, path traversal, session flaws, information exposure, broken access control), confirming exploitation with evidence, analysing the impact on Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA), and writing professional reports. You will learn to think like an attacker so you can build better defences. Every vulnerability has a CWE identifier (Common Weakness Enumeration) that categorises it, and every exploit has a real-world impact that must be measured. By the end of this course, you will be able to perform a complete vulnerability assessment on any Java-based web application.
theory.why_important: In 2023, the average cost of a data breach was $4.45 million. Most breaches exploit known vulnerability types — SQL injection, XSS, broken access control — that could have been found with a proper assessment. Companies hire penetration testers specifically to find these issues before criminals do. The OWASP Top 10 lists the most critical web application security risks, and every one of them can be found through systematic assessment. Whether you are a developer who wants to write secure code, a tester who needs to verify security, or an aspiring security professional, these skills are essential and in extremely high demand.

key_concepts.explanation: A vulnerability assessment follows a structured process: (1) Reconnaissance — understand what the application does, its features, roles, and pages. (2) Attack Surface Mapping — identify every input point: forms, URL parameters, cookies, headers, file uploads. (3) Vulnerability Testing — for each input, test for specific vulnerability types using known payloads. (4) Exploitation — confirm the vulnerability by demonstrating real impact. (5) Impact Analysis — assess the effect on Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability. (6) Documentation — write clear, reproducible evidence with screenshots and payloads. (7) Remediation — recommend specific fixes for each vulnerability.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of it like a health check-up for software. The doctor (you) examines every organ (input), runs specific tests (payloads), diagnoses problems (vulnerabilities), and prescribes treatment (fixes).

key_concepts.explanation: SQL injection occurs when user input is inserted directly into a SQL query without sanitisation, allowing an attacker to modify the query's logic. String injection uses quotes to break out of string context: ' OR '1'='1 makes a WHERE clause always true. Numeric injection does not need quotes: 1 OR 1=1. To test: enter a single quote (') in every input field — if you get a database error, SQL injection is likely possible. The fix is ALWAYS parameterised queries (prepared statements), never string concatenation.
key_concepts.ai_hint: SQL injection is like adding extra instructions to a shopping list. The cashier (database) follows ALL instructions on the list, including the ones the attacker added.

key_concepts.explanation: XSS occurs when user input is rendered in a web page without encoding, allowing an attacker to inject JavaScript that executes in other users' browsers. Reflected XSS: the payload is in the URL and reflected back immediately (e.g., search results). Stored XSS: the payload is saved in the database and displayed to every user who views the page (e.g., forum posts, profile fields). To test: enter <script>alert('XSS')</script> in every input field and check if an alert box appears. The fix is OUTPUT ENCODING — convert < to < before rendering.
key_concepts.ai_hint: XSS is like someone slipping a note into a book at the library. Every person who reads that page follows the note's instructions, thinking they came from the original author.

key_concepts.explanation: CSRF tricks a logged-in user's browser into making a request they did not intend. If a user is logged into their bank and visits a malicious page, that page can contain a hidden form that transfers money from the user's account. The browser automatically includes the session cookie, so the bank thinks it is a legitimate request. To test: check if sensitive forms include a CSRF token. If a form works without a token, it is vulnerable. The fix is anti-CSRF tokens — unique random values in each form that the server validates.
key_concepts.ai_hint: CSRF is like someone forging your signature on a cheque. The bank (server) sees your signature (cookie) and processes it, not knowing you did not write it.

key_concepts.explanation: Command injection occurs when user input is passed to a system command (like Runtime.exec() or ProcessBuilder) without sanitisation. The attacker appends extra commands using shell metacharacters like ; | & || && or backticks. For example, if an app runs ping <user_input>, an attacker can enter 127.0.0.1; cat /etc/passwd to execute an additional command. To test: append ; whoami or | ls to inputs that seem to be used in system commands. The fix is input validation (whitelist allowed characters) and avoiding system commands entirely when possible.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Command injection is like adding extra stops to a delivery driver's route. They follow ALL the directions, including the ones the attacker added.

key_concepts.explanation: Path traversal occurs when user input controls a file path, and the attacker uses ../ sequences to navigate outside the intended directory. If an app serves files like /files?name=report.pdf, an attacker can try /files?name=../../../etc/passwd to read sensitive system files. To test: replace filenames with ../../etc/passwd or ..\..\windows\system32\config\sam. The fix is canonicalise the path and verify it stays within the allowed directory.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Path traversal is like asking a librarian for a book but giving directions that lead outside the library into the staff-only records room.

key_concepts.explanation: Session flaws allow attackers to hijack, fixate, or predict user sessions. Common issues: predictable session IDs (session_1, session_2...), session IDs in URLs (visible in browser history and logs), sessions that do not expire, sessions not destroyed on logout, same session ID before and after login (fixation), and missing HttpOnly/Secure cookie flags. To test: examine session cookies for randomness, check if IDs change after login, try reusing a session after logout, check cookie attributes in browser dev tools.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Session testing is like checking if hotel keycards work after checkout. If your old keycard still opens the room, the hotel has a security problem!

key_concepts.explanation: Information exposure means the application reveals data that should be kept hidden: detailed error messages showing database structure or stack traces, directory listings exposing file structures, version numbers of software (Apache, Tomcat, Java version), debug information left in production, source code comments with credentials, and sensitive data in HTML hidden fields or JavaScript. To test: cause errors (enter invalid data, access nonexistent pages) and examine the error messages. Check page source for comments. Try common paths like /admin, /debug, /status.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Information exposure is like leaving your house plans on the front lawn. Burglars can study them to find the weakest entry point.

key_concepts.explanation: Broken access control means users can act outside their intended permissions. This includes: accessing other users' data by changing an ID in the URL (IDOR), accessing admin pages without admin role, performing actions without authorisation checks, and horizontal privilege escalation (user A accesses user B's data) or vertical escalation (regular user accesses admin functions). To test: log in as a normal user and try accessing admin URLs directly. Change IDs in URLs to other users' IDs. Check if API endpoints validate permissions.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Broken access control is like an office where anyone can walk into the CEO's office because the door is unlocked. The nameplate says 'CEO Only' but nobody actually checks.

key_concepts.explanation: Every vulnerability must be assessed for its impact on three security properties: Confidentiality (can the attacker READ sensitive data?), Integrity (can the attacker MODIFY data?), and Availability (can the attacker DISRUPT the service?). SQL injection impacts all three: read data (C), modify data (I), drop tables (A). XSS primarily impacts C (steal cookies) and I (deface pages). CSRF impacts I (modify data on victim's behalf). Rate each as HIGH, MEDIUM, or LOW to prioritise fixes.
key_concepts.ai_hint: CIA analysis is like assessing a break-in: Can the thief SEE your private documents (Confidentiality)? Can they CHANGE your records (Integrity)? Can they LOCK you out of your own house (Availability)?

key_concepts.explanation: Every vulnerability maps to a CWE (Common Weakness Enumeration) identifier. This provides a standard language for categorising weaknesses. Key CWEs for web assessment: CWE-89 (SQL Injection), CWE-79 (XSS), CWE-352 (CSRF), CWE-78 (OS Command Injection), CWE-22 (Path Traversal), CWE-200 (Information Exposure), CWE-548 (Directory Listing), CWE-287 (Improper Authentication), CWE-862 (Missing Authorisation), CWE-434 (Unrestricted File Upload), CWE-502 (Deserialisation of Untrusted Data), CWE-20 (Improper Input Validation). Using CWEs in reports shows professionalism and enables consistent tracking across organisations.
key_concepts.ai_hint: CWEs are like medical diagnosis codes. Instead of saying 'the patient is sick', a doctor says 'ICD-10: J06.9 (upper respiratory infection)'. CWEs give vulnerabilities their precise diagnosis.

key_concepts.explanation: A professional vulnerability report contains for each finding: (1) Title — clear name (e.g., 'SQL Injection in Login Form'). (2) CWE ID — standardised identifier. (3) Description — what the vulnerability is. (4) Reproduction Steps — exact inputs, URLs, and actions to reproduce. (5) Evidence — screenshots, payloads used, and server responses. (6) Impact — CIA analysis and severity rating. (7) Remediation — specific code fix recommendation. Good reports are reproducible — someone else should be able to follow your steps and see the same result.
key_concepts.ai_hint: A good vulnerability report is like a recipe — anyone should be able to follow it and get the same result. Steps, ingredients (payloads), and expected outcome.

key_concepts.explanation: Not all vulnerabilities are equally dangerous. Rate severity as CRITICAL, HIGH, MEDIUM, LOW, or INFORMATIONAL based on: exploitability (how easy is it to exploit?), impact (what damage can be done?), affected users (one user or all users?), and prerequisites (does the attacker need authentication?). SQL injection with full database access is CRITICAL. Information exposure via verbose errors is MEDIUM. Missing HTTPS on a static page is LOW. Prioritise fixes: CRITICAL first, then HIGH, then work down.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of severity like a fire alarm system: CRITICAL = building on fire (evacuate now). HIGH = smoke detected (investigate immediately). MEDIUM = fire extinguisher expired (fix this week). LOW = exit sign bulb dim (add to maintenance list).

complete_examples.title: Example 1: SQL Injection Detection and Exploitation
complete_examples.description: Finding and exploiting SQL injection in a login form
complete_examples.output:
=== SQL INJECTION ASSESSMENT ===

--- Normal Login ---
Query: SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='admin' AND password='adminPassword'
LOGIN SUCCESS: Welcome admin

--- Detection: Single Quote Test ---
Query: SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='admin'' AND password='test'
LOGIN FAILED: Invalid credentials
(If this causes an SQL error, injection is likely)

--- Exploit: Authentication Bypass ---
Query: SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='admin' OR '1'='1' --' AND password='anything'
LOGIN SUCCESS (SQL injection!): Admin access granted

--- Exploit: Union-based data extraction ---
Query: SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='' UNION SELECT username,password FROM users --' AND password=''
LOGIN FAILED: Invalid credentials
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Step 1: Normal login works as expected. Step 2: Single quote test — in a real app, this would cause an SQL syntax error, confirming injection. Step 3: The OR '1'='1' makes the WHERE clause always true, bypassing authentication. The -- comments out the rest of the query. This is the classic SQL injection authentication bypass.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What is the difference between string SQL injection and numeric SQL injection? When would you use 1 OR 1=1 instead of ' OR '1'='1?

complete_examples.title: Example 3: CSRF Vulnerability Check
complete_examples.description: Verifying if forms have CSRF protection
complete_examples.output:
=== CSRF ASSESSMENT ===
Form: Transfer Funds
  POST method: true
  State-changing: true
  CSRF token present: false
  VERDICT: VULNERABLE TO CSRF!

Form: Change Password
  POST method: true
  State-changing: true
  CSRF token present: true
  VERDICT: Protected (token found)

Form: Search
  POST method: false
  State-changing: false
  CSRF token present: false
  VERDICT: Low risk (not state-changing)
complete_examples.ai_explanation: The transfer form is POST and state-changing but has NO CSRF token — an attacker could trick users into making transfers. The password form is protected with a token. The search form is GET and read-only, so CSRF is low risk. Focus CSRF testing on POST forms that change state (transfers, profile updates, password changes, deletions).
complete_examples.ai_challenge: If a form has a CSRF token, is it automatically safe? What if the server does not actually validate the token? How would you test that?

complete_examples.title: Example 4: Command Injection Test Suite
complete_examples.output:
=== COMMAND INJECTION TESTING ===

Payload: 127.0.0.1
  Executed: ping -c 1 127.0.0.1
  RESULT: Normal execution

Payload: 127.0.0.1; whoami
  Executed: ping -c 1 127.0.0.1; whoami
  RESULT: Additional command executed! VULNERABLE

Payload: 127.0.0.1 | cat /etc/passwd
  Executed: ping -c 1 127.0.0.1 | cat /etc/passwd
  RESULT: Additional command executed! VULNERABLE

Payload: 127.0.0.1 && ls -la
  Executed: ping -c 1 127.0.0.1 && ls -la
  RESULT: Additional command executed! VULNERABLE

Payload: `whoami`
  Executed: ping -c 1 `whoami`
  RESULT: Additional command executed! VULNERABLE

Payload: $(cat /etc/passwd)
  Executed: ping -c 1 $(cat /etc/passwd)
  RESULT: Additional command executed! VULNERABLE

Payload: 127.0.0.1 || id
  Executed: ping -c 1 127.0.0.1 || id
  RESULT: Normal execution
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Six different command injection techniques are tested. The semicolon, pipe, AND chain, backtick, and dollar substitution all allow injecting additional commands. The OR chain (||) only executes the second command if the first FAILS. Professional testers try ALL of these because some might be filtered while others are not.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Why might ; whoami be blocked but `whoami` still work? Think about what characters a basic filter might check for.

complete_examples.title: Example 5: Path Traversal Exploit
complete_examples.description: Testing path traversal with various encoding techniques
complete_examples.output:
=== PATH TRAVERSAL TESTING ===
Base directory: /var/www/uploads

  Input: report.pdf                             -> Path: /var/www/uploads/report.pdf          [Safe]
  Input: ../../../etc/passwd                     -> Path: /etc/passwd                           [TRAVERSAL!]
  Input: ..%2f..%2f..%2fetc%2fpasswd            -> Path: /var/www/uploads/..%2f...             [Safe]
  Input: ....//....//etc/passwd                  -> Path: /etc/passwd                           [TRAVERSAL!]
  Input: /etc/passwd                             -> Path: /etc/passwd                           [TRAVERSAL!]
  Input: uploads/../../../etc/passwd             -> Path: /etc/passwd                           [TRAVERSAL!]
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Classic ../ traversal works. URL-encoded ..%2f may or may not work depending on whether the server decodes before or after path resolution. Double-dot bypass (....//  becomes ../ after filter removes ../) can evade basic filters. Absolute paths bypass relative restrictions entirely. The canonical path check is the proper fix — resolve the full path and verify it starts with the allowed directory.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What would happen if the server decodes URL encoding BEFORE checking for path traversal? How would that change which payloads work?

complete_examples.title: Example 6: Session Management Audit
complete_examples.output:
=== SESSION MANAGEMENT AUDIT ===

Session ID Analysis:
  [PASS] ID length >= 16 characters
  [PASS] ID appears random (not sequential)
  [PASS] ID not in URL (cookie-based)

Cookie Security:
  [PASS] HttpOnly flag set
  [PASS] Secure flag set
  [PASS] SameSite attribute set

Session Lifecycle:
  [PASS] New ID generated after login
  [PASS] Absolute timeout configured
  [FAIL] Idle timeout configured
  [FAIL] Session destroyed on logout

SCORE: 8/10
RATING: FAIR

CRITICAL: Sessions persist after logout!
WARNING: No idle timeout — abandoned sessions stay active.
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This audit checks 10 session security properties. 8 pass but 2 critical issues found: sessions are not destroyed on logout (an attacker with a stolen session ID can use it even after the user logs out) and no idle timeout (sessions left open on shared computers remain active indefinitely). Both are real vulnerabilities found in production applications.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: If you found the 'session not destroyed on logout' issue, what would you write as the Reproduction Steps in your vulnerability report?

complete_examples.title: Example 7: Information Exposure Scanner
complete_examples.description: Detecting information leaks in server responses
complete_examples.output:
=== INFORMATION EXPOSURE SCAN ===

Error page: 2 issue(s)
  [FOUND] Stack trace exposed (reveals language, files, line numbers)
  [FOUND] SQL error exposed (reveals database type, query structure)

Normal page: CLEAN

Commented page: 1 issue(s)
  [FOUND] Sensitive HTML comment found

Server header: 1 issue(s)
  [FOUND] Server version disclosed (helps attacker target known CVEs)

Upload dir: 1 issue(s)
  [FOUND] Directory listing enabled (CWE-548)
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Five different information exposure types detected. Error pages are the most common leak — they reveal database type, file names, and line numbers. Server version headers help attackers find known CVEs. HTML comments often contain developer notes with sensitive information. Directory listings expose the file structure.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Write the information exposure for the error page as a vulnerability report entry with CWE, description, CIA impact, and remediation.

complete_examples.title: Example 8: CIA Impact Calculator
complete_examples.description: Rating vulnerabilities by Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability impact
complete_examples.output:
=== CIA IMPACT ASSESSMENT ===

SQL Injection (Login Bypass)
  C: HIGH    I: HIGH    A: HIGH    -> CRITICAL
  Read all data, modify records, drop tables

Stored XSS (Profile Field)
  C: HIGH    I: MEDIUM  A: LOW    -> HIGH
  Steal cookies, deface pages, no server impact

CSRF (Fund Transfer)
  C: LOW     I: HIGH    A: LOW    -> MEDIUM
  Cannot read data, but modifies balances

Path Traversal (Read-only)
  C: HIGH    I: LOW     A: LOW    -> MEDIUM
  Read /etc/passwd and config files, cannot modify

Missing Session Expiry
  C: MEDIUM  I: MEDIUM  A: LOW    -> MEDIUM
  Extended access window if session stolen

Server Version Disclosure
  C: LOW     I: LOW     A: LOW    -> LOW
  Helps plan attacks but no direct impact
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Each vulnerability is rated across all three CIA dimensions. SQL injection is CRITICAL because it impacts all three at HIGH level. XSS is HIGH because of cookie theft (C) but cannot crash the server (A). CSRF modifies data (I) but cannot read it (C). Path traversal reads files (C) but in this case cannot write. The combined score determines overall severity.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Could a path traversal vulnerability ever have HIGH Integrity impact? Think about what happens if the attacker can WRITE files through the traversal.

complete_examples.title: Example 9: Broken Access Control Detector
complete_examples.description: Testing for horizontal and vertical privilege escalation
complete_examples.output:
=== ACCESS CONTROL ASSESSMENT ===

Vertical Escalation (user accessing admin pages):
  user1    (USER ) -> /dashboard     : Expected access
  user1    (USER ) -> /admin         : VULNERABILITY: No role check!
  user1    (USER ) -> /admin/users   : VULNERABILITY: No role check!
  user1    (USER ) -> /reports       : VULNERABILITY: No role check!
  admin    (ADMIN) -> /admin         : Expected access

Horizontal Escalation (IDOR - accessing other users' data):
  user1    -> data 1 (owner: user1   ): Own data (OK)
  user1    -> data 2 (owner: user2   ): OTHER USER'S DATA (IDOR!)
  user2    -> data 1 (owner: user1   ): OTHER USER'S DATA (IDOR!)
  user1    -> data 3 (owner: admin   ): OTHER USER'S DATA (IDOR!)
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Two types of broken access control found. Vertical: user1 (normal user) can access /admin, /admin/users, and /reports — pages meant only for admins. Horizontal: user1 can access user2's data and even admin's data by changing the ID parameter. Both are in the OWASP Top 10 as #1 vulnerability — Broken Access Control.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: If you found 3 admin pages accessible to normal users, how would you prioritise them? Consider which page allows the most damage.

complete_examples.title: Example 10: Vulnerability Report Generator
complete_examples.description: Generating a professional vulnerability report entry
complete_examples.output:
============================================================
VULNERABILITY #1: SQL Injection in Login Form
============================================================
CWE:      CWE-89
Severity: CRITICAL
Location: /login POST endpoint, 'username' parameter

DESCRIPTION:
  The login form concatenates user input directly into a SQL query without parameterisation.

REPRODUCTION STEPS:
  1. Navigate to /login
  2. Enter username: admin' OR '1'='1' --
  3. Enter any password
  4. Click Login
  5. Observe: logged in as admin

PAYLOAD: admin' OR '1'='1' --
EVIDENCE: Dashboard page displayed with admin privileges

CIA IMPACT:
  Confidentiality: HIGH - Full database read access
  Integrity:       HIGH - Can INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE records
  Availability:    HIGH - Can DROP tables

REMEDIATION:
  Replace string concatenation with PreparedStatement using parameterised queries.
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This is a professional-grade vulnerability report entry. It includes every required element: CWE identifier, severity rating, exact location, clear description, numbered reproduction steps, the exact payload used, evidence of exploitation, full CIA impact analysis, and a specific remediation recommendation. Anyone reading this can reproduce the finding.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Write a similar report entry for a Stored XSS vulnerability found in a user profile 'about me' field.

complete_examples.title: Example 11: Complete Vulnerability Scanner Simulation
complete_examples.description: Automated testing of multiple vulnerability types against an endpoint
complete_examples.output: === AUTOMATED VULNERABILITY SCAN === Target: Sample Web Application FINDINGS: 11 potential vulnerabilities [CWE-89] SQL Injection in /login (username) payload: ' OR '1'='1 [CWE-89] SQL Injection in /login (username) payload: 1 OR 1=1 [CWE-89] SQL Injection in /login (username) payload: '; DROP TABLE users; -- [CWE-79] XSS in /login (username) payload: [CWE-79] XSS in /login (username) payload: [CWE-79] XSS in /search (q) payload: [CWE-79] XSS in /search (q) payload: [CWE-78] Command Injection in /ping (host) payload: ; whoami [CWE-78] Command Injection in /ping (host) payload: | ls [CWE-78] Command Injection in /ping (host) payload: && cat /etc/passwd SUMMARY BY TYPE: CWE-89: 3 finding(s) CWE-79: 4 finding(s) CWE-78: 3 finding(s)
complete_examples.ai_explanation: The scanner tests each endpoint with multiple payload types. It finds SQL injection in the login (string concatenation), XSS in login and search (no output encoding), and command injection in ping (uses Runtime.exec). A real scanner would actually submit these payloads and check responses. This simulation shows the methodology: test every input with every relevant payload type.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: This scanner has false positives (findings that might not actually be exploitable). How would you verify each finding manually?

complete_examples.title: Example 12: Attack Surface Mapper
complete_examples.description: Identifying all input points in a web application
complete_examples.output:
=== ATTACK SURFACE MAP ===
Application: Sample Banking App

PAGE         TYPE            NAME         METHOD TESTS
----------------------------------------------------------------------
/login       form_field      username     POST   Test: SQLi, XSS
/login       form_field      password     POST   Test: SQLi
/search      query_param     q            GET    Test: XSS, SQLi
/profile     query_param     id           GET    Test: IDOR, SQLi
/transfer    form_field      to_account   POST   Test: CSRF, SQLi
/transfer    form_field      amount       POST   Test: CSRF, integer overflow
/upload      file_upload     document     POST   Test: file type, path traversal
/ping        form_field      host         POST   Test: command injection
/download    query_param     file         GET    Test: path traversal
Cookie       cookie          session      AUTO   Test: fixation, expiry, flags

Total input points: 10
Priority targets: /login (auth bypass), /transfer (financial), /download (file access)
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Before testing, map EVERY input point. This app has 10 inputs across 7 pages. Each input has suggested tests based on its type: form fields for injection, query params for IDOR and traversal, file uploads for type restrictions, cookies for session flaws. The priority targets are those with the highest impact if compromised.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What other input types might exist that are not listed here? Think about HTTP headers (Referer, User-Agent), hidden form fields, and API endpoints.

complete_examples.title: Example 13: Mitigation Verification
complete_examples.description: Testing that security fixes actually work
complete_examples.output:
=== MITIGATION VERIFICATION ===
  SQL Injection              Before: VULN  After: Safe  -> FIX VERIFIED
  XSS (script tag)           Before: VULN  After: Safe  -> FIX VERIFIED

All fixes verified successfully!
complete_examples.ai_explanation: After applying fixes, you MUST verify they work. Test with the same payloads that exploited the vulnerability before. If the fix works, the payload should no longer succeed. This is regression testing for security — ensuring fixes are effective and do not break normal functionality.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What else should you test after applying a fix? Think about: bypass techniques, similar payloads, and whether normal functionality still works.

visual_diagram.title: Web Application Vulnerability Assessment: Complete Methodology
visual_diagram.content: [VULNERABILITY ASSESSMENT METHODOLOGY] PHASE 1 RECONNAISSANCE Browse the app, understand features, roles, and pages Map: Login, Profile, Search, Transfer, Admin, Upload, Download | v PHASE 2 ATTACK SURFACE MAPPING List ALL inputs: form fields, URL params, cookies, headers, files Each input = potential attack vector to test | v PHASE 3 VULNERABILITY TESTING For each input, test: SQLi ' OR '1'='1 | 1 OR 1=1 | UNION SELECT XSS <script>alert(1)</script> | <img onerror=alert(1)> CSRF Check forms for anti-CSRF tokens CMDi ; whoami | ls | && cat /etc/passwd PATH ../../../etc/passwd | ..%2f encoding IDOR Change ?id=1 to ?id=2 | access other users data INFO Error messages, directory listings, comments, headers | v PHASE 4 EXPLOITATION & EVIDENCE Confirm each finding is real (not a false positive) Capture: payload used, server response, screenshots | v PHASE 5 CIA IMPACT ANALYSIS Confidentiality: Can attacker READ sensitive data? Integrity: Can attacker MODIFY data? Availability: Can attacker DISRUPT the service? | v PHASE 6 REPORT & REMEDIATION For each finding: CWE ID, reproduction steps, evidence, CIA, fix Prioritise: CRITICAL -> HIGH -> MEDIUM -> LOW [CWE QUICK REFERENCE] CWE-89 SQL Injection CWE-79 Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) CWE-352 Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) CWE-78 OS Command Injection CWE-22 Path Traversal CWE-284 Broken Access Control CWE-200 Information Exposure CWE-434 Unrestricted File Upload [REMEMBER] 1. Map EVERY input before testing anything 2. Test each input with MULTIPLE payload types 3. CONFIRM exploits — no false positives in reports 4. Always assess CIA impact for prioritisation 5. Write REPRODUCIBLE steps — anyone must be able to follow 6. Recommend SPECIFIC fixes, not just "fix it"

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 13: Fix SQL Injection
practice_exercises.description: Refactor vulnerable code to use PreparedStatement.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: SQL injection is still the #1 database attack. Let's find it!

hints.message: Replace string concatenation with ? placeholders and setString().
PreparedStatement ps = conn.prepareStatement("SELECT...WHERE username=? AND password=?"); ps.setString(1,user); ps.setString(2,pass);
Full fix: PreparedStatement with ? for both parameters, setString for each, executeQuery on ps.

False condition: Profile loaded (no content)
Time-based: (5 second delay, then profile loaded)

Conclusion: VULNERABLE - different responses for true/false conditions confirms blind SQLi

hints.message: If the app responds DIFFERENTLY to true vs false conditions, blind SQLi is confirmed.
True condition shows content, false condition doesn't = the SQL is being evaluated.
Different responses for AND '1'='1 vs AND '1'='2 = blind SQLi confirmed. Time delay also confirms.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 17: UNION-Based Data Extraction
practice_exercises.description: Use UNION SELECT to extract data from other tables.

Expected: Shows username and password columns from users table

hints.message: UNION SELECT must have the same number of columns as the original query.

payload = "' UNION SELECT username, password FROM users --"

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 18: Test for Second-Order SQLi
practice_exercises.description: Identify SQL injection that triggers on stored data.

practice_exercises.expected_output:

Later query: SELECT * FROM profiles WHERE username='admin'--'
Effect: Returns admin's profile (-- comments out closing quote)
Why dangerous: The injection happens LATER, not at input time. Input validation at registration may pass but the stored value is malicious.

hints.message: Second-order means the payload is stored first and triggers later in a different query.
The username admin'-- is valid text to store, but when used in a query later, the -- comments out the rest.
Input seems safe when stored. Danger comes when the stored value is used in another query without parameterisation.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 19: Write SQLi Remediation
practice_exercises.description: Recommend specific fixes for SQL injection findings.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: SQL injection is still the #1 database attack. Let's find it!

hints.message: The fix is ALWAYS PreparedStatement with ? placeholders.
Pattern 1: setString for string input. Pattern 2: setInt for numeric. Pattern 3: project-wide replacement.
PreparedStatement for all. Use setString/setInt based on data type. Never concatenate user input into SQL.

";

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 30: XSS in Different Output Contexts

127.0.0.1 && cat /etc/passwd -> INJECTION DETECTED

$(id) -> INJECTION DETECTED

hints.message: Shell metacharacters like ; | && ` $() all enable command chaining.
Each character has a different effect: ; = sequential, | = pipe, && = conditional, ` = substitution.
All except the normal IP are detected. 5 different injection techniques identified.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 34: Fix Command Injection
practice_exercises.description: Refactor code to prevent command injection.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: These attacks are sneaky but very powerful!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Testing fix:
192.168.1.1 -> true
192.168.1.1; whoami -> false

hints.message: Whitelist allowed characters: only digits and dots for an IP address.
Regex ^[0-9]{1,3}(\.[0-9]{1,3}){3}$ allows only valid IP format.
Validate input with whitelist regex. Reject anything that doesn't match. Never pass raw input to exec().

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 35: Path Traversal Detection
practice_exercises.description: Test if a file download endpoint is vulnerable.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: These attacks are sneaky but very powerful!

report.pdf -> Safe (/var/www/files/report.pdf)
../../../etc/passwd -> TRAVERSAL! (/etc/passwd)
....//....//etc/passwd -> TRAVERSAL! (/etc/passwd)
subdir/file.txt -> Safe (/var/www/files/subdir/file.txt)

hints.message: Path traversal uses ../ to escape the intended directory.
getCanonicalFile() resolves all ../ sequences. Check if result starts with base dir.
2 of 4 escape the base directory. Fix: canonicalize and verify startsWith(baseDir).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 36: Fix Path Traversal
practice_exercises.description: Implement safe file access with canonical path validation.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: These attacks are sneaky but very powerful!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
ALLOWED: /uploads/doc.pdf
BLOCKED: Path traversal detected

hints.message: Use getCanonicalFile() to resolve the full path, then check startsWith().
File base = new File(baseDir); File f = new File(base, filename).getCanonicalFile(); if(!f.getPath().startsWith(base.getCanonicalPath())) block;
Canonicalize both base and requested path. If requested doesn't start with base -> block.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 58: Remediation Recommendations
practice_exercises.description: Write specific fix recommendations for each vulnerability type.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: You are putting it all together now — a complete professional assessment!
